name: Cron Chain + Symlink Race (Insane)
story: You are investigating a busy operations server where automation has replaced manual administration. Several cron jobs move archives and publish reports, and each job runs under a different account. At first, the upload archive process only seems to grant access to a backup user, but that account can influence files consumed by a second root-owned publisher. Your mission is to chain the weaknesses: first pivot through tar wildcard injection, then win a check-then-use symlink race to write a root-trusted file and recover the flag.
objectives: Exploit wildcard injection in a cron-executed tar command to pivot from student to backup, then abuse a root symlink race to gain root and capture /root/flag.txt.

steps.title: Map scheduled tasks
steps.description: Enumerate /etc/crontab and identify the script executed each minute as backup user.
steps.question: What is the full path of the cron script executed as user backup?

hints.text: Find the entry running every minute with user backup.

steps.title: Pivot to backup
steps.description: Exploit wildcard argument injection in tar to execute commands as backup and obtain a reusable shell context.
steps.question: After successful pivot, which user context do you gain?

hints.text: Inspect /opt/ops/archive-uploads.sh and look for tar with wildcard expansion.
Use crafted file names with --checkpoint and --checkpoint-action.

steps.title: Find the race target
steps.description: Analyze the root publisher script and identify the destination file affected by a check-then-use gap.
steps.question: Which destination file path is vulnerable to the symlink race?

hints.text: The script checks -L first, then sleeps, then writes.

steps.title: Win root and capture flag
steps.description: Use the race to write a sudoers rule as root, switch back to student, then read /root/flag.txt.
steps.question: What is the captured flag?

hints.text: Make root write attacker-controlled report content into /etc/sudoers.d via symlink.
Payload content can grant student NOPASSWD sudo.
After the sudoers file appears, use sudo to read /root/flag.txt.
